perturbation.original_text: Before doing so, each Party must ensure that affiliates and Representatives are required to protect the Confidential Information on terms consistent with this Agreement .
perturbation.changed_text: Before doing so, each Party should encourage affiliates and Representatives to protect the Confidential Information on terms generally consistent with this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text creates a legal *obligation* to *ensure* affiliates and representatives are *required* to protect confidential information. By changing 'must ensure' to 'should encourage' and 'required' to 'generally consistent,' the obligation becomes vague and difficult to enforce, making compliance discretionary.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3426.1 outlines the potential for injunctive relief to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets. By weakening the protective measures required of affiliates and representatives to a mere 'encouragement' of 'generally consistent' terms, the modified text undermines the ability to prevent trade secret misappropriation, potentially conflicting with the remedies available under the UTSA. The change from 'ensure' to 'encourage' weakens the obligation to take affirmative steps to protect the information, making the agreement unenforceable and in violation of California law. The change from 'required' to 'generally consistent' does not provide the same protection for confidential information as required by law.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially conflicting with remedies available under California law. The original text mandates that each party "must ensure" affiliates and representatives are "required" to protect confidential information. The changed text weakens this to "should encourage" affiliates and representatives to protect confidential information on terms "generally consistent." This change from a mandatory to a discretionary obligation undermines the ability to prevent trade secret misappropriation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 (CIV 3426.1) defines 'misappropriation' and refers to 'a duty to maintain secrecy.' By making the protection of confidential information more discretionary and less stringent, the modified text contradicts the legal intent to prevent misappropriation as defined in California law.

perturbation.original_text: Each of us acknowledges that money damages may not be sufficient compensation for a breach of this Agreement. Each of us agrees that the other may seek court orders to stop Confidential Information from becoming public in breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of us acknowledges that money damages might not be sufficient compensation for a breach of this Agreement. Each of us agrees that the other can consider seeking court orders to potentially stop Confidential Information from becoming public in breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that 'money damages may not be sufficient' and 'each of us agrees that the other may seek court orders'. The modified text weakens this to 'money damages might not be sufficient' and 'each of us agrees that the other can consider seeking court orders'. The changes makes pursuing injunctive relief optional and uncertain, weakening the protection of confidential information. This introduces ambiguity in the enforcement of the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3426.2 allows for injunctive relief to prevent the actual or threatened misappropriation of trade secrets. By making the pursuit of court orders to stop the disclosure of confidential information discretionary ('can consider seeking') rather than a right ('may seek'), the modified text weakens the ability to prevent misappropriation, conflicting with the intent of the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA). This makes the enforcement of confidentiality uncertain, rendering the agreement less effective under California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.2 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.2. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) If the court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity regarding the enforcement of trade secret protection, contradicting California Civil Code § 3426.2. The original text provides a stronger assertion of the right to seek injunctive relief ('may seek'), while the changed text weakens this to a discretionary consideration ('can consider seeking'). According to the law snippet, 'Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined,' suggesting a clear legal avenue for injunctive relief. The modified text's ambiguity undermines this right, making enforcement uncertain and potentially conflicting with the law's intent to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party may disclose the other’s Confidential Information to its Representatives (who may then disclose that Co nfidential Information to other of its Representatives) only if those Representatives are required to have the Confidential Information for the purpose s in this Agreement. Before doing so, each Party must ensure that affiliates and Representatives are required to protect the Confidential Information on terms consistent with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party may disclose the other’s Confidential Information to its Representatives (who may then disclose that Confidential Information to other of its Representatives) if those Representatives have a reasonable need to know the Confidential Information. Before doing so, each Party should attempt to have affiliates and Representatives protect the Confidential Information on terms they find acceptable.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires representatives to be 'required to have' the information for the purposes in the agreement. It also requires that the party 'must ensure' affiliates and representatives are 'required to protect' the information. The modified text changes this to 'reasonable need to know' and 'should attempt to have' affiliates 'protect the Confidential Information on terms they find acceptable.' This weakens the requirement for disclosure and makes the protection discretionary, potentially leading to breaches of confidentiality without clear recourse.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 16600 generally prohibits agreements that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While this section primarily relates to non-compete agreements, the protection of trade secrets is an exception. By weakening the obligation to protect confidential information and allowing representatives to determine the terms of protection themselves, the modified text can indirectly harm a company's ability to protect its trade secrets. This undermines the trade secret protections, making it more difficult to prevent unfair competition.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification:
The modified text introduces a contradiction by weakening the protection of confidential information, potentially conflicting with California Business and Professions Code § 16600, which concerns restraints of trade and, by extension, the protection of trade secrets. The original contract mandated that representatives 'are required to have' the information for agreed purposes and 'must ensure' protection, while the modified version uses 'reasonable need to know' and 'should attempt to have' protection on 'terms they find acceptable.'

Snippet 1 from BPC 16600 states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' Although primarily about non-compete agreements, this law acknowledges that trade secret protection is an exception. By significantly weakening the obligation to protect confidential information, the modified text can harm a company's ability to protect its trade secrets. This weakening undermines the protection against unfair competition.

perturbation.original_text: To take reasonable steps to protect the other Party’s Confidential Information. These steps must be at least as protective as those we take to protect our own Confidential Information of a similar nature ;
perturbation.changed_text: To consider reasonable steps to protect the other Party’s Confidential Information. These steps should ideally be similar to those we consider adequate to protect our own Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires 'reasonable steps' that are 'at least as protective' as the party's own measures. The revised text changes this to 'consider reasonable steps' that 'should ideally be similar' to those considered 'adequate'. The terms “consider,” “ideally,” and “adequate” introduce subjectivity, making it difficult to prove a breach of the confidentiality agreement. The steps are no longer mandatory or required to be as protective.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3426.1 allows for injunctive relief for misappropriation of trade secrets. By changing the obligation to 'take reasonable steps' to the weaker 'consider reasonable steps' and replacing the objective 'at least as protective' with the subjective 'should ideally be similar' and 'adequate,' the modification weakens the protection of confidential information. This makes it more difficult to prove that the receiving party failed to take sufficient measures to protect the information, undermining the legal basis for seeking an injunction to prevent misappropriation under California law. This change introduces ambiguity and weakens the enforceability of the confidentiality provisions, making it more difficult to seek legal recourse under the UTSA.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity that weakens the protection of confidential information, making it more difficult to prove a breach, contradicting the intent of California Civil Code § 3426.1. The original contract language mandated specific protective measures. The changed language uses subjective terms ('consider,' 'ideally,' 'adequate') that dilute the obligation, impacting the ability to seek injunctive relief under the UTSA, as misappropriation becomes harder to demonstrate. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4, highlights the importance of maintaining secrecy, which the altered text undermines through its weaker and more subjective language.
